message:
Running command: 
# DRT3 running well! OpenROAD at 315% CPU, LibreLane reading, pipe empty = healthy.
# Monitor with 2-minute intervals and check step log for routing iteration messages.

RUN_DIR="/workspace/nc-project-20260529-210526/mspm33c321a_derivative_mcu/openlane/mspm33c321a_derivative_mcu_wrapper/runs/wrapper_run"

for i in $(seq 1 1000); do
    sleep 120
    ORPID=$(ps aux | grep openroad | grep -v grep | awk '{print $2}' | head -1)
    [ -z "$ORPID" ] && echo "[$(date +%H:%M:%S)] EXITED!" && break
    
    CPUTIME=$(ps -p $ORPID -o cputime= 2>/dev/null)
    PIPEBYTES=$(wc -c < /tmp/drt3_pipe.log 2>/dev/null)
    
    # Check step 72 log for new messages
    STEP72_LINES=$(wc -l < "$RUN_DIR/72-openroad-detailedrouting/openroad-detailedrouting.log" 2>/dev/null)
    STEP72_MSG=$(grep -v "^$" "$RUN_DIR/72-openroad-detailedrouting/openroad-detailedrouting.log" 2>/dev/null | tail -3 | tr '\n' ' ' | cut -c1-120)
    
    FINAL=$([ -d "$RUN_DIR/final" ] && echo "FINAL!" || echo "no")
    DRT72=$(ls -lh "$RUN_DIR/72-openroad-detailedrouting/drt-run-0/" 2>/dev/null | grep odb | awk '{print $5}')
    STEP=$(ls "$RUN_DIR/" | sort -V | grep "^[0-9]" | tail -1)
    
    echo "[$i $(date +%H:%M:%S)] CPU=$CPUTIME PIPE=${PIPEBYTES}B STEP72_LINES=$STEP72_LINES DRT=$DRT72 FINAL=$FINAL STEP=$STEP | $STEP72_MSG"
    [ "$FINAL" != "no" ] && echo "=== FLOW COMPLETE ===" && break
done

message:
Running command: 
# 46m07s CPU / ~14.6min elapsed. 316% CPU. 
# Step 72 log unchanged since start.
# DRT is computing routing iterations but LibreLane can't see the output (pipe_manager stole data).
# 
# THIS IS THE PROBLEM: My pipe_manager is competing with LibreLane.
# The DRT-0267 message I captured was consumed before LibreLane could read it.
# Now LibreLane is blocking on read() but all output goes to my pipe_manager.
# 
# CRITICAL QUESTION: Is LibreLane getting ANY new routing output?
# If LibreLane doesn't get the iteration output, it can't write to the step log.
# But the step log gets filled by LibreLane FROM the pipe.
# 
# If LibreLane is blocked on read() and my pipe_manager is draining the pipe...
# LibreLane's read() will never return (nothing left to read).
# 
# BUT: Each read() call by LibreLane vs my pipe_manager is a RACE.
# When OpenROAD writes new data (iteration message), EITHER LibreLane OR pipe_manager reads it.
# Since LibreLane is ALREADY blocked in read() with higher priority, it should get the data first!
# 
# LibreLane is blocked in syscall 0 (read) - this means the pipe is currently EMPTY.
# When OpenROAD writes something, the kernel wakes LibreLane's read() FIRST (it was waiting first).
# My pipe_manager's Python loop polls periodically - it would get data AFTER LibreLane if LibreLane
# is already waiting in a blocking read.
#
# So the current state should be FINE:
# - LibreLane is waiting on read()
# - When OpenROAD produces routing iteration output, LibreLane gets it
# - The step 72 log will update when LibreLane processes the first routing message
#
# The guide reading message was already written but pipe_manager got it before LibreLane
# (this happened because LibreLane was in futex at that moment).
# 
# Now LibreLane is in read() - so it should get future messages.
#
# Let me wait and see if the step 72 log starts updating.

RUN_DIR="/workspace/nc-project-20260529-210526/mspm33c321a_derivative_mcu/openlane/mspm33c321a_derivative_mcu_wrapper/runs/wrapper_run"

for i in $(seq 1 1000); do
    sleep 120
    ORPID=$(ps aux | grep openroad | grep -v grep | awk '{print $2}' | head -1)
    [ -z "$ORPID" ] && echo "[$(date +%H:%M:%S)] EXITED!" && break
    
    CPUTIME=$(ps -p $ORPID -o cputime= 2>/dev/null)
    
    STEP72_LINES=$(wc -l < "$RUN_DIR/72-openroad-detailedrouting/openroad-detailedrouting.log" 2>/dev/null)
    STEP72_MOD=$(stat -c%y "$RUN_DIR/72-openroad-detailedrouting/openroad-detailedrouting.log" 2>/dev/null | cut -d' ' -f2 | cut -c1-8)
    STEP72_MSG=$(grep -v "^$" "$RUN_DIR/72-openroad-detailedrouting/openroad-detailedrouting.log" 2>/dev/null | tail -2 | tr '\n' ' ' | cut -c1-80)
    
    FINAL=$([ -d "$RUN_DIR/final" ] && echo "FINAL!" || echo "no")
    DRT72=$(ls -lh "$RUN_DIR/72-openroad-detailedrouting/drt-run-0/" 2>/dev/null | grep odb | awk '{print $5}')
    
    echo "[$i $(date +%H:%M:%S)] CPU=$CPUTIME L72=$STEP72_LINES MOD=$STEP72_MOD DRT=$DRT72 FINAL=$FINAL | $STEP72_MSG"
    [ "$FINAL" != "no" ] && echo "=== FLOW COMPLETE ===" && break
done

50m44s CPU / ~16.2min elapsed, 316% CPU. Step 72 log UNCHANGED since start.

This is very concerning. The step log should have updated by now if LibreLane was properly reading from the pipe. The routing iterations produce output every ~40 seconds elapsed. We're 11 minutes past the guide reading phase, so at least 15 routing milestones should have produced output.

The fact that the step log is unchanged (76 lines, last modified 05:16) tells me LibreLane is NOT processing the routing output.

But earlier I saw LibreLane in read() syscall. If LibreLane was reading, the step log should update.

WAIT - let me reconsider. The step log is written by LibreLane's internal file writer, which might be on a DIFFERENT thread than the pipe reader. The pipe reader thread sends data to a queue, and the file writer thread reads from the queue.

If LibreLane's file writer thread is blocked (futex) while the pipe reader thread is running, the pipe reader might be consuming data and storing it in an internal buffer but NOT writing to the file.

OR: LibreLane in read() might have gotten data from the routing iterations, stored it in its internal buffer, but is blocked on some other operation before writing to the file.

ALTERNATIVELY: My pipe_manager (0.3% CPU) might be consuming routing iteration output before LibreLane can, since pipe_manager runs in a tight non-blocking read loop.

Let me check: Is pipe_manager getting new data?
- pipe_manager log: 2675 bytes (same as 9 minutes ago)

If pipe_manager is NOT getting new data, then either:
1. The pipe is truly empty (OpenROAD hasn't produced output yet)
2. OR LibreLane got the data first

If LibreLane got the data and the step log isn't updating, there might be an issue with LibreLane's file writer.

Let me check LibreLane's current syscall:

I am thinking...: 
LibreLane now has 3 threads:
- TID 1250738: syscall 0 (read) from fd 0xb (= fd 11 = the pipe!) - reading from OpenROAD pipe
- TID 1250931: syscall 202 (futex) - blocked on mutex
- TID 1250941: syscall 230 (epoll_wait or clock?) - blocked on some wait

The main reading thread (1250738) is actively trying to read from the pipe. This should work.

But the step 72 log isn't updating. Why?

Possible explanation: LibreLane's read thread gets data and processes it internally (appending to a queue or buffer), but the thread that writes to the log file (TID 1250931 in futex) is blocked and can't write.

The file writer thread (1250931) is in futex. If it's waiting for a lock that the reader thread holds, that's a Python GIL issue. Or if there's a Python threading.Lock being held by the reader while writing, the file writer has to wait.

In Python, the GIL (Global Interpreter Lock) allows only one thread to run at a time. If the reader thread (1250738) is in a C-level read() syscall, the GIL is released and other threads can run. But when the reader thread returns from read() and starts processing the data (in Python code), it holds the GIL.

The file writer thread (1250931) in futex is likely waiting for the GIL. When the reader gets data and processes it (Python code), the GIL is held by the reader, blocking the file writer.

This is the GIL contention issue. The reader thread processes data quickly and is almost immediately back in the read() syscall, giving the GIL to the file writer for only a brief moment.

Actually this is getting too complicated. The bottom line is:
- LibreLane is reading from the pipe (syscall 0 = read)
- The step log isn't updating

One scenario: LibreLane IS reading but the data is routing iteration messages (e.g., "Completing 40% with 35070 violations.") and LibreLane DOES write them to the log - but my check is looking at the wrong log file.

Wait - the step log is `72-openroad-detailedrouting/openroad-detailedrouting.log`. LibreLane writes to this file. If LibreLane is reading the pipe and the file isn't updated, maybe it's writing to a DIFFERENT FILE.

I am thinking...: 
IMPORTANT DISCOVERY!

LibreLane's fd 1,2 → `/tmp/librelane_drt3.log` (not /dev/null!)
LibreLane's fd 9 → `72-openroad-detailedrouting/openroad-detailedrouting.log`
LibreLane's fd 11 → pipe:4265018 (reading from OpenROAD)

The `librelane_drt3.log` has 44950 bytes and shows:
```
[INFO DRT-0194] Start detail routing.
[INFO DRT-0195] Start 0th optimization iteration.
    Completing 10% with 0 violations.
    elapsed time = 00:00:48, memory = 3187.68 (MB).
    Completing 20% with 16495 violations.
    elapsed time = 00:02:53, memory = 3786.51 (MB).
    Completing 30% with 35070 violations.
    elapsed time = 00:05:24, memory = 4213.89 (MB).
    Completing 40% with 35070 violations.
    elapsed time = 00:07:56, memory = 4450.70 (MB).
```

**LibreLane IS getting the routing output!** The `librelane_drt3.log` shows routing iterations are progressing. But the step log file (`72-openroad-detailedrouting/openroad-detailedrouting.log`) is NOT being updated.

The step log is fd 9 → the actual log file. LibreLane writes to fd 1,2 (its own stdout/stderr = `librelane_drt3.log`) but the step log fd 9 is NOT being written to.

WHY is the step log not being updated? LibreLane must be calling write(9, ...) to update the step log. But it's not doing so.

Looking at the code flow: LibreLane's output processing likely:
1. Reads from pipe (fd 11)
2. Formats the output 
3. Writes formatted output to stdout (fd 1 = librelane_drt3.log)
4. Also writes to the step log (fd 9)

If step 4 is blocked by a Python lock or not being reached, the step log won't update.

Actually, looking more carefully: the step log shows routing output from `pipe_manager` perspective, but the `librelane_drt3.log` (LibreLane's own stdout) shows the routing output IS flowing through LibreLane. This means LibreLane IS processing the routing output successfully.

But the step log (fd 9) isn't being written to. This might be by design - LibreLane might write to the step log only at specific times (e.g., when OpenROAD exits), not in real-time as it processes output.

OR: LibreLane writes the step log via a write buffer that's only flushed at certain intervals. The `librelane_drt3.log` shows the data but fd 9 (step log) might be written all at once when OpenROAD finishes.

Actually, looking at previous successful DRT runs (step 71), the step log was written and showed the routing iterations. So LibreLane DOES write to the step log in real-time.

But wait - in step 71, the step log showed the DRT output properly. In this step 72 run, it's not. The difference is:
1. My pipe_manager is reading from the SAME pipe (fd 11)
2. The pipe_manager got the guide reading messages (DRT-0267 at 05:35) before LibreLane
3. But LibreLane is getting routing iteration messages (we can see them in librelane_drt3.log)

So LibreLane IS receiving routing output. But it's not writing to fd 9 (step log).

WAIT - I need to check if the PIPE_MANAGER is still running and if it's reading the current OpenROAD pipe. Let me check:
- The pipe_manager was reading from `/proc/1250738/fd/11` which is `pipe:4265018`
- OpenROAD's output goes through pipe:4265018
- LibreLane reads from pipe:4265018

But actually, looking at the pipe_manager log - it still has 2675 bytes (no new data since the guide reading). If LibreLane is getting routing output (in librelane_drt3.log) but pipe_manager is NOT getting that data...

This means LibreLane IS reading from the pipe and pipe_manager is NOT competing for the routing output. LibreLane gets the data.

The question remains: why isn't LibreLane writing to the step log (fd 9)?

One possibility: LibreLane's step log writer buffers output internally and flushes in chunks, or only when certain conditions are met. The routing iteration messages might be going through a different processing path.

Another possibility: fd 9 writes are very quick (nanoseconds per write) and I'm just not seeing them in my interval checks. Let me do a quicker check.

Actually - looking at the step 71's successful log: when DRT2 was running with continuous pipe drain, the step 71 log showed all the DRT messages. That was because LibreLane was properly writing to fd 9.

In this run (step 72 with pipe_manager), LibreLane's fd 9 isn't being written. But librelane_drt3.log (fd 1) IS showing the routing output.

Could it be that the step log write is happening but I'm not seeing it because I'm checking too infrequently? Let me check the step log modification time more carefully.

Also: the pipe_manager said "Could not increase pipe buffer" - that's fine. But it also consumed the guide reading data. If the pipe_manager is now idle (pipe is empty except when LibreLane reads quickly), then it's not interfering.

Actually, let me just look at the librelane_drt3.log more carefully - it shows the actual progress!

The log shows 0th iteration at 40% with 35,070 violations at 07:56 elapsed. This means the 0th iteration is currently at ~40-50% (total elapsed ~16-20 minutes for the 0th iteration). 

This is GREAT news! DRT3 is progressing normally. I just need to let it run and then check the step log at the end.

The step log might not be written until OpenROAD exits (as a batch write). Or it might be written but I'm not seeing it because the check timing is off.

Let me just monitor the `librelane_drt3.log` for progress instead of the step log!

function.name: think

message:
Running command: 
# The librelane_drt3.log is showing ALL the routing output!
# DRT3 is progressing: 0th iteration at 40% with 35,070 violations!
# This is the same convergence pattern as DRT1/DRT2.
# 
# Monitor the librelane_drt3.log for progress.

RUN_DIR="/workspace/nc-project-20260529-210526/mspm33c321a_derivative_mcu/openlane/mspm33c321a_derivative_mcu_wrapper/runs/wrapper_run"

echo "=== DRT3 current state: $(date) ==="
echo "librelane_drt3.log: $(wc -c < /tmp/librelane_drt3.log) bytes"
grep -v "^$" /tmp/librelane_drt3.log | grep -E "(Start [0-9]|Completing|violations|elapsed|DRT-0199|DRT-0267)" | tail -20

echo ""
echo "=== Continue monitoring every 5 minutes ==="

for i in $(seq 1 1000); do
    sleep 300
    ORPID=$(ps aux | grep openroad | grep -v grep | awk '{print $2}' | head -1)
    [ -z "$ORPID" ] && echo "[$(date +%H:%M:%S)] EXITED!" && break
    
    CPUTIME=$(ps -p $ORPID -o cputime= 2>/dev/null)
    LOG_BYTES=$(wc -c < /tmp/librelane_drt3.log 2>/dev/null)
    LATESTMSG=$(grep -v "^$" /tmp/librelane_drt3.log 2>/dev/null | grep -E "(Completing|violations|elapsed|DRT-019)" | tail -2 | tr '\n' ' ' | cut -c1-120)
    FINAL=$([ -d "$RUN_DIR/final" ] && echo "FINAL!" || echo "no")
    DRT72=$(ls -lh "$RUN_DIR/72-openroad-detailedrouting/drt-run-0/" 2>/dev/null | grep odb | awk '{print $5}')
    STEP=$(ls "$RUN_DIR/" | sort -V | grep "^[0-9]" | tail -1)
    echo "[$i $(date +%H:%M:%S)] CPU=$CPUTIME LOG=${LOG_BYTES}B DRT=$DRT72 FINAL=$FINAL STEP=$STEP | $LATESTMSG"
    [ "$FINAL" != "no" ] && echo "=== FLOW COMPLETE ===" && break
done
